meta.description: CGI/IUGS controlled vocabulary of stratigraphic rank terms covering chronostratigraphic, lithostratigraphic, and biostratigraphic units according to the International Stratigraphic Guide (ICS). Used in GeoSciML GeologicUnit rank descriptions.

concepts.definition: A body of rock established as a distinct entity in the classification of the Earth's rocks based on any of the various stratigraphic procedures (lithostratigraphic, chronostratigraphic, biostratigraphic, etc.). Top concept.
The largest division of geologic time, defined by the International Commission on Stratigraphy. The Phanerozoic and Proterozoic are eons. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is eonothem.
A subdivision of geologic time larger than an eon. The Precambrian is informally used as a supereon. Rarely used in formal stratigraphic classification.
A division of geologic time that is a subdivision of an eon. Examples include the Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eras. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is erathem.
A subdivision of geologic time that is a subdivision of an era. Examples include the Cretaceous, Jurassic, and Triassic periods. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is system.
A subdivision of geologic time that is a subdivision of a period. Examples include the Early Cretaceous, Late Jurassic. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is series.

The smallest subdivision of geologic time in formal chronostratigraphic classification, subdividing an epoch. The corresponding chronostratigraphic unit is stage. Examples: Aptian, Albian, Cenomanian.
A subdivision of an age in geochronologic classification. Rarely used formally. Corresponds to substage in chronostratigraphy.
A body of strata characterized by a particular fossil taxon or assemblage, or by a geochemical signature. Used in biostratigraphic, chemostratigraphic, and magnetostratigraphic classification.
The body of rocks formed during the time represented by a polarity chron or biozone; the smallest formal unit of chronostratigraphic classification above the bed level.
The fundamental unit of chronostratigraphic classification, representing rock formed during an age. Examples: Aptian Stage, Albian Stage. Defined by boundary stratotype sections (GSSPs).
A subdivision of a stage in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to subage in geochronologic terminology.
A chronostratigraphic unit larger than a system, corresponding to an erathem. Rarely used in current practice.
A chronostratigraphic unit corresponding to a period in geochronology. The fundamental unit of the geological time scale. Examples: Cretaceous System, Jurassic System.
A subdivision of a system in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to a subdivision of a period. Used in some regional stratigraphic schemes.
A chronostratigraphic unit larger than a series, grouping two or more series. Corresponds to a subdivision of an erathem.
A chronostratigraphic unit corresponding to an epoch in geochronology. Represents rock deposited during an epoch. Examples: Lower Cretaceous Series, Upper Jurassic Series.
A subdivision of a series in chronostratigraphic classification. Corresponds to a subdivision of an epoch.
A lithostratigraphic unit comprising two or more groups with significant lithological or genetic unity. Used for major basin-filling sequences. Examples: Gondwana Supergroup.
A lithostratigraphic unit comprising two or more associated formations with significant lithological unity. Defines a major depositional cycle or phase within a basin.
A lithostratigraphic unit that is a subdivision of a group, comprising two or more formations. Used when a group needs further subdivision.

The fundamental unit of lithostratigraphic classification. A body of rock strata with consistent lithological characteristics that make it possible to recognize and map it at the surface or in the subsurface. Examples: Barra Velha Formation, Carapebus Formation.

A lithostratigraphic unit that is a named subdivision of a formation, distinguished by a particular lithological character. Not all formations are divided into members; a member occurs only within a single formation.
The smallest lithostratigraphic unit, representing a single layer of rock with observable internal physical homogeneity. Beds are the fundamental units of sedimentary sequences.

A body of rock that is defined and characterized on the basis of its lithological properties and stratigraphic position. The hierarchy includes supergroup, group, subgroup, formation, member, and bed.
A body of rock defined by its fossil content (biozone), characterized by the occurrence of one or more particular taxa. Biozones are the fundamental biostratigraphic units used for correlation.

A body of rock characterized by a distinctive geochemical signature (isotopic ratios, trace element concentrations) used for correlation and paleoenvironmental reconstruction. Examples: carbon isotope excursion units, strontium isotope zones.
A body of rock defined by its remanent magnetization polarity. Magnetic polarity zones (magnetozones) are used for correlation with the global geomagnetic polarity timescale (GPTS).
